title: The Incarnation
html:
Big Question

How far is God willing to go to be with you?

It was Christmas Eve 1223 in the small village of Greccio, Italy. A small, ragged man, with hands marked by the stigmata of Christ, had asked for something strange: he wanted to celebrate the Christmas Mass in a way that had never been done before. That man was Francis of Assisi. He asked for an ox, a donkey, a manger with hay, and a figure of a child. And so, in a cave on the mountainside, before peasants who arrived carrying torches, Francis recreated the birth of Jesus.

Thomas of Celano, his first biographer, tells that when Francis stood before the manger and began to read the Gospel, his voice broke. He could not say the name of Bethlehem without weeping. Because Francis had understood something that theologians debate but that he lived: God did not send instructions from heaven, did not issue a decree, did not remain on his throne. He entered. He became fragile. He became flesh.

The name of that village, Greccio, sounds like «grace.» And that is what Francis celebrated that night: the grace of a God who becomes small enough to be a baby who depends on his mother to eat, to sleep, to survive. How far is God willing to go to be with you? All the way to flesh. All the way to the manger. All the way to the cross.

Activity

Read John 1:14 aloud: «And the Word became flesh and dwelt among us.» Now replace «dwelt» with its literal meaning in Greek: «pitched his tent,» «camped among us.» Draw a tent and inside it write: «God camps with me.» What does it mean that God pitched his tent in the middle of our camp?

The expression «Word» (in Greek Logos) points to the Second Person of the Trinity, the eternal Word of the Father, through whom all things were made (Jn 1:3). That same Word, through whom suns and galaxies exist, became a baby who depends on his mother to eat, to sleep, to survive. Saint Paul describes it in a stunning hymn: Christ, though he was in the form of God, «emptied himself, taking the form of a servant, being born in the likeness of men» (Phil 2:7) (CCC 461).

«Why did the Word become flesh?»

The Catechism answers this question with four intertwined reasons (CCC 457-460):

First, to save us. The Son of God became man so that, as man, he could offer perfect reparation for our sins. In Christ, God himself takes up our cause and pays the price we could not pay. «The Son of Man came not to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many» (Mk 10:45).

Second, so that we might know God’s love. The Cross is the definitive demonstration of how far God’s love reaches. If you doubt that God loves you, look at the manger and the crucifix: in both, God gives himself away. «In this the love of God was made manifest among us, that God sent his only Son into the world, so that we might live through him» (1 Jn 4:9).

Connection

Think of the person who has loved you most in this life. How far did they go for you? Now think of God: he became a body capable of suffering, of being betrayed, of being nailed to a Cross. What does that tell you about the seriousness of his love? Is there anything you could do that would make God love you less?

Third, to be our model of holiness. «Learn from me, for I am meek and humble of heart» (Mt 11:29). Jesus does not only tell us how to live; he shows us how to live. In him we see how to love, how to forgive, how to suffer, how to pray. He is «the way, the truth, and the life» (Jn 14:6) (CCC 459).

Fourth, to make us partakers of the divine nature. Saint Athanasius sums it up: «God became man so that man might become God.» Not that we become gods by nature, but that by grace we are elevated to divine filiation. «By his precious promises he has granted us to become partakers of the divine nature» (2 Pet 1:4) (CCC 460).

The Annunciation: The Yes That Changed History

The moment the Word becomes flesh is the Annunciation. The archangel Gabriel comes to Nazareth, to a virgin named Mary, betrothed to Joseph (Lk 1:26-27). He greets her with words never before spoken to a human creature: «Hail, full of grace, the Lord is with you» (Lk 1:28). Mary is troubled, but Gabriel explains: she will conceive a son by the power of the Holy Spirit, and this son will be the Son of God (CCC 484-486).

Mary asks: «How can this be, since I have no husband?» (Lk 1:34). Her question is not one of disbelief like Zechariah’s, but of wonder. She believes it is possible but wants to know how. The angel responds: «The Holy Spirit will come upon you, and the power of the Most High will overshadow you» (Lk 1:35). This is the language of creation: just as the Spirit hovered over the waters at the beginning (Gen 1:2), so now it overshadows Mary so that a new creation may take place within her (CCC 487).

Reflection

Mary did not ask to be the Mother of the Savior. God simply proposed it and she accepted. There were no conditions, no «but...» Just one word: «Let it be done.» Salvation entered the world through a «yes.» How many times do we miss God’s graces because we respond with a «maybe» or «later» or «no»? Is there something God is asking of you today that you need to say «yes» to?

And then Mary pronounces the words that change history: «Behold, I am the handmaid of the Lord; let it be done to me according to your word» (Lk 1:38). At that moment, the eternal Word becomes flesh in her womb. All of creation had been waiting for this instant. The entire Old Testament — the promises to Abraham, the liberation from Egypt, the prophecies of Isaiah — converge in that «yes» spoken in a humble house in Nazareth (CCC 488-494).

True God and True Man

The faith of the Church from the earliest centuries affirms that Jesus Christ is true God and true man. He is not a half-and-half mixture: not a demigod, not a man elevated to divine status. He is fully God and fully man. The Council of Chalcedon (AD 451) defined it with precision: Christ is «one Person in two natures,» without confusion, without change, without division, without separation (CCC 467).

This means that Jesus has everything proper to God — eternity, omnipotence, infinite wisdom — and everything proper to man — a body, hunger, thirst, weariness, pain, death. In a single Person, the Second Person of the Trinity, the divine nature and the human nature are united (CCC 468-469).

Going Deeper

How can Jesus be true God and true man at the same time? It is a mystery our finite minds cannot fully grasp. But the Catechism gives us help: the union of the two natures in Christ does not mean that the divine and the human are mixed like water and wine, but that they remain distinct without being separated. As iron heated by fire remains iron but participates in the heat of the fire, so Christ’s humanity participates in divinity without ceasing to be fully human (CCC 470-471).

This truth has enormous practical consequences. Because Jesus is true God, his words are the words of God, and his love is God’s love made visible. Because he is true man, he knows what it means to suffer, to feel alone, to be afraid, to be tempted. As the Letter to the Hebrews says: «We have not a high priest who is unable to sympathize with our weaknesses, but one who in every respect has been tempted as we are, yet without sin» (Heb 4:15) (CCC 474-477).

Mary’s Divine Motherhood

If Jesus is true God and true man, then Mary is truly the Mother of God. Thus proclaimed the Council of Ephesus (AD 431) by giving Mary the title of Theotokos — «the one who gives birth to God.» Not because Mary generated divinity, but because she conceived and gave birth to the Person of Jesus, who is God (CCC 495).

Mary is ever-virgin: she conceived by the power of the Holy Spirit without the intervention of a man. The Church professes that Mary was a virgin before, during, and after the birth of Jesus (CCC 499). Her perpetual virginity is not a rejection of marriage, but a sign that the conception of Jesus is exclusively God’s work, a new creation. As the Catechism says: «Christ’s birth did not diminish his mother’s virginal integrity but consecrated it» (CCC 499).

Connection

Look up Luke 1:26-38. Read the dialogue between the angel and Mary. Notice the angel’s words: «Full of grace.» This means Mary was preserved from original sin from the first moment of her conception (the Immaculate Conception). Why? Because she was going to be the Mother of the Son of God. The one who would save us was prepared by God with the best possible dwelling. Write «Full of grace» on a piece of paper, and below it write what that phrase tells you about Mary’s dignity.

Mary is not a decorative figure in the history of salvation. Her fiat (her «let it be done») is the model of every response of faith. In her we see how to receive God: with total availability, without reserve, with complete trust. That is why the Church calls her «Mother of the Church» and honors her with filial love (CCC 501, 963-964).

Biblical Connection: The Annunciation

In the sixth month the angel Gabriel was sent from God to a city of Galilee named Nazareth, to a virgin betrothed to a man whose name was Joseph, of the house of David; and the virgin’s name was Mary. And he came to her and said, «Hail, full of grace, the Lord is with you!»

But she was greatly troubled at the saying, and considered in her mind what sort of greeting this might be. And the angel said to her, «Do not be afraid, Mary, for you have found favor with God. And behold, you will conceive in your womb and bear a son, and you shall call his name Jesus. He will be great, and will be called the Son of the Most High; and the Lord God will give to him the throne of his father David, and he will reign over the house of Jacob for ever; and of his kingdom there will be no end.»

And Mary said to the angel, «How can this be, since I have no husband?» And the angel said to her, «The Holy Spirit will come upon you, and the power of the Most High will overshadow you; therefore the child to be born will be called holy, the Son of God.»

And Mary said, «Behold, I am the handmaid of the Lord; let it be done to me according to your word.» Luke 1:26-31, 34-35, 38

The account of the Annunciation is one of the most beautiful and dense passages in all of Scripture. In it, heaven and earth intersect, promise and fulfillment meet, human freedom and divine grace converge. God does not impose the Incarnation; he proposes it. And he awaits the free response of a young woman who could have said no. That is why Christian tradition has seen in Mary the new Eve: just as through one woman (Eve) disobedience entered, through one woman (Mary) the obedience that saves us entered (CCC 494, 511).

For Reflection

  1. «The Word became flesh» (Jn 1:14) — God became matter, became vulnerable. What does this tell you about the value of your own body and of created matter?
  2. Mary answered «let it be done» without knowing everything that would come after. In what areas of your life is it hard to give that «yes» to God without having control over what will happen?
  3. Jesus is true God and true man. How does it help you to know that Jesus experienced weariness, loneliness, pain, and temptation just as you do?
  4. «God became man so that man might become God» (Saint Athanasius). What change would it imply in your life to take seriously that you are called to share in the divine nature?
  5. Mary is a model of faith because she trusted without seeing the complete plan. Is there a situation in your life where you need to trust like Mary, without having all the answers?

Key Takeaway

Key Takeaway: The infinite God became finite flesh so that you, made of flesh, might share in his infinite life.

Cultural Resources

«The Annunciation» by Fra Angelico — One of the most beautiful frescoes of the Renaissance, painted in the convent of San Marco in Florence (c. 1440). Fra Angelico captured the moment with a supernatural light: the angel and Mary are in a portico overlooking an enclosed garden (a symbol of Mary’s virginity). The colors are soft, Mary’s posture is one of humble reception. The faces of the angel and Mary seem to radiate a peace not of this world. It is a visual meditation on the mystery: in the silence of the scene, the Word becomes flesh.

Film «Jesus of Nazareth» by Franco Zeffirelli (1977) — Considered by many the best cinematic production on the life of Christ. The Annunciation scene is particularly moving: Mary (played by Olivia Hussey) appears serene yet human, and the encounter with Gabriel is filmed with a light that seems to come from another dimension. The entire film is an excellent tool for visualizing the mystery of the Incarnation in its historical context.

«God Became Man,» Themes of Christian Faith from Opus Dei — A clear and profound text available on opusdei.org that explains the dogma of the Incarnation in accessible language. It covers the Annunciation, Mary’s divine motherhood, the hypostatic union, and the consequences for our life of faith. Excellent for those who want to go deeper after class.
